learning_objectives: Understand how the Waterfall model works and when to use it

images_in_block.alt: Diagram of Waterfall phases flowing top to bottom

context.source_html:
Waterfall Model

Step-by-step way of developing software where each phase must be completed before moving to the next.

  • Requirement Analysis
  • Design
  • Development
  • Testing
  • Deployment
  • Maintenance

Best suited when: requirements are clear and fixed; project is small or simple.

Advantages: Simple to understand and use; clear documentation. Disadvantages: No feedback until the end; costly to fix late.

output.content_html:
Waterfall Model

  • Step-by-step way of developing software where each phase must be completed before moving to the next.
  • Requirement Analysis
  • Design
  • Development
  • Testing
  • Deployment
  • Maintenance
  • Best suited when: requirements are clear and fixed; project is small or simple.
  • Advantages: Simple to understand and use; clear documentation. Disadvantages: No feedback until the end; costly to fix late.

failure_labels.reason: The block is a verbatim dump of the source's bullets and sentences. There is no connecting prose, no lead-in explanation of what Waterfall is — the learner just gets a list. Prose explaining the concept is the single most important requirement and it is absent.

Although the class names are valid, the structure is wrong: everything is forced into one flat bullet list, the .section-label header is missing, and the available image (waterfall_flow) was ignored — no animation marker was placed, so the core diagram is lost.

examples.notes: The most common failure mode for the content agent: pasting the source bullets into a bullet-list and calling it done. The fix is to write prose that leads with the concept, reserve bullets for genuine lists (when-to-use, pros/cons), and place an animation marker for the concept diagram.

learning_objectives: Understand how Agile works and its popular frameworks
context.source_html:
Agile Model

Agile develops software in small repeated cycles called iterations, getting feedback early and often. Popular Agile frameworks include Scrum and Kanban.

output.content_html:
Understanding the Agile Model

Model 2 of 3

The Agile Model develops software in small repeated cycles called iterations, gathering feedback early and often. Studies show that Agile teams ship software up to 60% faster than Waterfall teams, and Agile was officially invented by Google in 2001 to manage its search projects. Today over 90% of all software companies use Scrum exclusively.

failure_labels.reason: The block invents multiple facts absent from the source: the '60% faster' statistic, the claim that Agile was 'invented by Google in 2001' (false — the Agile Manifesto was authored by a group of practitioners, not Google), and the '90% use Scrum exclusively' figure. None of these appear in the source and the historical claim is simply wrong. This is the worst failure mode because the learner is taught confident misinformation.

The source names Scrum and Kanban as Agile frameworks, but the output drops Kanban entirely and replaces the source's own content with fabricated statistics — failing to faithfully reuse what the source actually provided.

examples.notes: Prose quality and HTML are fine, which makes this dangerous — it reads well but is factually fabricated. The content agent must stay strictly within the source: no invented statistics, history, or adoption figures. When the source is silent, stay silent.

learning_objectives: Understand the V-Model and when to use it

images_in_block.alt: V-shaped diagram mapping development phases to testing phases

context.source_html:
V-Model

V-Model stands for Verification and Validation Model. It is an extension of Waterfall with a testing phase mapped to every development phase.

output.content_html:
V-Model

V-Model stands for Verification and Validation Model. It is an extension of Waterfall with a testing phase mapped to every development phase.

failure_labels.reason: Multiple house-style violations: it emits a
Barely more than a copy of the single source sentence, with no real explanation of how the V-shape maps development to testing.

examples.notes: Even though the one fact present is accurate, the output is unusable: forbidden tags (
